food_name: Banh Mi with Pate and Cha Lua (Vietnamese Ham Baguette)
aliases: Banh Mi with Ham and Pate
Baguette with Ham (Banh Mi Pate Cha Lua)

english: Banh Mi with Ham and Pate
Baguette with Ham (Banh Mi Pate Cha Lua)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Banh Mi; 'sandwich food product' (FOODON_03315595) is the closest defensible parent class. A more specific Vietnamese banh mi term is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Sandwich) is the closest defensible match. No specific Banh Mi or Vietnamese baguette sandwich code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Banh Mi or Vietnamese baguette sandwich in the current release.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard banh mi sandwich weighing approximately 300 g, comprising a full Vietnamese-style baguette filled with cha lua, pate, pickled vegetables, cucumber, coriander, chilli, and condiments.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Sandwich composite and component ingredient data (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA entry exists for Banh Mi Pate Cha Lua. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for French baguette (FDC ID 172688), pork liver pate (FDC ID 167903), Vietnamese-style pork sausage (closest: cooked pork sausage), and fresh vegetables. The input record values (energy 183 kcal, protein 8.33 g, fat 10 g, carbohydrate 15 g per 100 g) are consistent with a weighted composite of these components at typical banh mi filling ratios (~50% baguette, ~25% cha lua/pate, ~25% vegetables/condiments) and are retained as the best available estimate. Singapore HPB database does not contain a direct Banh Mi entry. FAO/INFOODS SEA does not contain a specific Banh Mi record.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008); white French baguette analogous GI reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Banh Mi Pate Cha Lua as a composite dish. GI of 72 is estimated using the University of Sydney GI Database value for white French baguette (GI ~72, high category), which constitutes approximately 50% of the dish by weight and dominates the glycemic response. The pate, cha lua, and vegetable components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the composite GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI x carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (72 x 45.0) / 100 = 32.4, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 15.0 x 3 = 45.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 400 mg per 100 g (1200 mg per full 300 g serving) from processed cha lua, pork liver pate, soy sauce, and mayonnaise, with low potassium offset.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its very high sodium load of approximately 1200 mg per serving; if consumed, reduce cha lua and pate portions, omit soy sauce, and increase fresh vegetable fillings to partially offset sodium with potassium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white baguette base (GI ~72) with 45 g carbohydrate and glycemic load of 32.4 per serving, combined with low dietary fibre (3 g per serving) and saturated fat from pate that may worsen insulin resistance.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, substitute a wholegrain or high-fibre bread for the white baguette, reduce pate and mayonnaise, and increase non-starchy vegetable fillings to lower the glycemic load and improve postprandial glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combination of high sodium (400 mg/100 g), saturated fat (3.33 g/100 g), trans fat (0.17 g/100 g), and cholesterol (33.33 mg/100 g) from processed pork products and pate, with no meaningful omega-3 or fibre offset.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of this dish due to its high sodium, saturated fat, and trans fat content from processed pork sausage and pate; if eaten occasionally, reduce processed meat portions, omit high-sodium condiments, and pair with a fibre-rich side to mitigate cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory processed pork products (cha lua, pate) rich in saturated fat and arachidonic acid (omega-6), combined with chilli (nightshade) and absence of anti-inflammatory omega-3s or polyphenols.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise this dish; processed pork sausage and pate are pro-inflammatory, chilli is a nightshade that may exacerbate symptoms in sensitive individuals, and the dish provides no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bánh Mì Pâté Chả Lụa (Banh Mi with Pate and Cha Lua) is a classic Vietnamese baguette sandwich widely sold in Vietnam and across Southeast Asia, including Singapore. The dish features a light, crispy French-influenced baguette — a legacy of French colonial influence on Vietnamese cuisine — split lengthwise and filled with cha lua (Vietnamese steamed pork sausage wrapped in banana leaf), pork liver pate, sliced cucumber, pickled daikon and carrot (do chua), fresh coriander (cilantro), sliced chilli, mayonnaise, and soy sauce or Maggi seasoning. The baguette provides a crunchy exterior and soft interior, while cha lua contributes a mild, slightly springy savoury flavour and pate adds richness and umami depth. The pickled vegetables provide acidity and crunch that balance the fatty filling. Nutritionally, a 300 g serving delivers approximately 549 kcal, 25 g protein, 30 g fat, and 45 g carbohydrate, with high sodium (~1200 mg) from processed pork products and condiments. The white baguette base has a high glycemic index (~72) and a glycemic load of approximately 32.4 per serving, making this dish unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without modification. The dish is not halal due to pork content. It is a common breakfast and lunch street food in Vietnam and is available at Vietnamese bakeries and food courts in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a banh mi sandwich?
Is banh mi suitable for diabetics?
What is in a Vietnamese baguette sandwich?
How much sodium is in banh mi?
What is the glycemic index of banh mi?
Is banh mi good for high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact approved-source entry exists for Banh Mi Pate Cha Lua as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for French baguette, pork liver pate, and cooked pork sausage at typical banh mi filling ratios. The input record values are consistent with this component-based estimate and are retained. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database value for white French baguette as the dominant carbohydrate source. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and absence of a direct approved-source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Vietnamese sandwich dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024; component-level MUFA/PUFA data for cha lua and pate are not reported in available approved sources. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not reported in available approved sources; estimated potassium is approximately 150 mg/100 g based on component analogy but is not confirmed by an approved source and is therefore set to -1. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024 for the specific Banh Mi Pate Cha Lua formulation. All core macros (protein_g 8.33, fat_g 10.0, carbohydrate_g 15.0) are non-zero and supported by component-based estimation from USDA FoodData Central. No zero-macro justification is required. The dish contains pork and is not halal. Country of origin in the input record is listed as Singapore, reflecting the local serving context; the dish originates from Vietnam. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white French baguette as the dominant carbohydrate contributor per the University of Sydney GI Database. Multilingual note: This is a Vietnamese dish; mandatory local-language requirement per G6 and Section 7.2 is Vietnamese only. Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not mandatory for Vietnamese cuisine and are correctly set to null. The ontology match types have been corrected to 'closest_parent' (from 'approximate') to conform to the allowed values: exact, closest_parent, none.
